steps.title: Beroepen
steps.desc: Welcome to B1. You can already talk about the past, make plans, and give opinions. Now you enter the world of work, money, and real Dutch life. We start with the vocabulary of professional life — the words you'll hear in every kantoor and vergadering.
goals: Talk about what people do for a living

steps.example: Wat is je beroep?
steps.exampleEn: What is your profession?
steps.note:
Formal word for job/profession.
'Baan' = job (specific position).
'Werk' = work (general).

steps.example: Ik werk op een kantoor.
steps.exampleEn: I work at an office.
steps.note:
Het-word. 'Op een kantoor' = at an office.
NOT 'in een kantoor' — Dutch uses 'op' here.
Plural: kantoren.

steps.example: Mijn collega's zijn aardig.
steps.exampleEn: My colleagues are nice.
steps.note:
De-word. Plural: collega's (with apostrophe!).
Dutch uses apostrophe for plurals
of words ending in -a, -o, -u, -y.

steps.example: Mijn baas is streng maar eerlijk.
steps.exampleEn: My boss is strict but fair.
steps.note:
De-word. Also: chef, leidinggevende (manager).
The Dutch workplace is quite flat —
many people call their boss by first name.

steps.example: Hij werkt bij een groot bedrijf.
steps.exampleEn: He works at a large company.
steps.note:
Het-word. 'Bij een bedrijf werken' = work at a company.
Also: 'de firma' (the firm), 'het bureau' (the office/agency).

steps.example: De klant heeft altijd gelijk.
steps.exampleEn: The customer is always right.
steps.note:
De-word. Plural: klanten.
'De klant heeft altijd gelijk' — same saying as English!

steps.title: 📐 Ik Werk Als...
steps.text:
To say what someone does for a living:

Ik werk als leraar. = I work as a teacher.
Zij werkt als arts. = She works as a doctor.
Hij werkt als ingenieur. = He works as an engineer.

Alternative with zijn (no article!):
Ik ben leraar. = I am a teacher.
Zij is arts. = She is a doctor.

Remember from Unit 1: no article before professions!
'Ik ben EEN leraar' = WRONG.
'Ik ben leraar' = CORRECT.

steps.example: Zij werkt als arts in het ziekenhuis.
steps.exampleEn: She works as a doctor in the hospital.
steps.note:
De-word. More formal than 'dokter'.
'De huisarts' = the GP (general practitioner).
Very common in the Netherlands: everyone has a huisarts.

steps.example: De leraar geeft les.
steps.exampleEn: The teacher gives a lesson.
steps.note:
De-word. 'De docent' = more formal (university level).
'Les geven' = to teach (lit: to give lessons).

steps.also: 'De baan' also = the track/lane (ice skating, racing).
steps.example: Ik zoek een nieuwe baan.
steps.exampleEn: I'm looking for a new job.
steps.note:
De-word. 'Een baan zoeken' = to look for a job.
'Een baan hebben' = to have a job.
More specific than 'werk' (work in general).

steps.example: Mijn werkgever betaalt goed.
steps.exampleEn: My employer pays well.
steps.note:
De-word. werk + gever = work + giver.
'De werknemer' = the employee (work + taker).
Transparent compound — you can see the logic!

steps.title: 🏢 Common Professions
steps.text:
de advocaat = the lawyer
de ingenieur = the engineer
de verpleegkundige = the nurse
de accountant = the accountant
de programmeur = the programmer
de kok = the cook
de journalist = the journalist
de politieagent = the police officer
de ondernemer = the entrepreneur
de ambtenaar = the civil servant

Many are cognates — you can guess most of them!
The Netherlands has lots of ZZP'ers
(zelfstandigen zonder personeel = freelancers).

steps.q: 'Ik werk op een kantoor.' — why 'op' and not 'in'?
steps.hint: Op een kantoor, op school, op de universiteit

steps.s: Zij werkt ___ arts in het ziekenhuis.

steps.q: 'Mijn collega's' — why the apostrophe?

steps.s: Ik ben ___. (teacher — no article!)
steps.hint: No article before professions with zijn

steps.q: 'De werkgever' literally means:

steps.s: Mijn {1} werkt bij een groot {2}.
steps.hint: My colleague works at a large company.

steps.title: Mijn Werkdag
steps.desc: Now that you know the people and places, let's learn the daily rhythm of working life. Meetings, breaks, deadlines, and emails — the bread and butter of any Dutch kantoor.

steps.example: De vergadering begint om tien uur.
steps.exampleEn: The meeting starts at ten.
steps.note:
De-word. Formal meeting.
'Overleg' = consultation/discussion (less formal).
'Een vergadering hebben' = to have a meeting.

steps.example: We hebben om twee uur overleg.
steps.exampleEn: We have a discussion at two.
steps.note:
Het-word. Less formal than 'vergadering'.
Often used for team check-ins.
'Overleggen' = to discuss/consult (verb).

steps.example: Laten we pauze nemen.
steps.exampleEn: Let's take a break.
steps.note:
De-word. 'Lunchpauze' = lunch break.
'Pauze nemen' = to take a break.
Dutch office lunch = usually 30 min, often at your desk.

steps.example: Dit project duurt drie maanden.
steps.exampleEn: This project lasts three months.
steps.note:
Het-word. Same as English!
'Aan een project werken' = to work on a project.
'De deadline' = also used in Dutch (borrowed from English).

steps.also: Also means: an agreement/deal between people.
steps.example: Ik heb een afspraak om drie uur.
steps.exampleEn: I have an appointment at three.
steps.note:
De-word. 'Een afspraak maken' = to make an appointment.
Polysemy: appointment AND agreement.
'We hebben een afspraak.' = We have a deal.

steps.example: Ik heb je e-mail gelezen.
steps.exampleEn: I read your email.
steps.note:
De-word. Also written as 'de mail'.
'Een e-mail sturen' = to send an email.
'Mailen' = to email (verb). Very common.

steps.example: Het regende, daarom bleef ik thuis.
steps.exampleEn: It rained, that's why I stayed home.
steps.note:
Connector! Cause → result.
'Daarom' = that's why (points to result).
V2 rule: 'Daarom bleef ik thuis.' (verb second!)

steps.example: Ik kan niet komen, ik ben namelijk ziek.
steps.exampleEn: I can't come, you see, I'm sick.
steps.note:
Adverb, NOT a conjunction! Goes INSIDE the clause.
'Ik ben namelijk ziek.' (after verb, before complement)
Used to explain a reason — very common in Dutch.

steps.title: 📅 Een Typische Werkdag
steps.text:
08:30 — Ik kom op kantoor. = I arrive at the office.
09:00 — We hebben overleg. = We have a meeting.
10:30 — Ik beantwoord e-mails. = I answer emails.
12:00 — Lunchpauze. = Lunch break.
13:00 — Ik werk aan een project. = I work on a project.
14:00 — We hebben een vergadering. = We have a meeting.
15:30 — Koffiepauze. = Coffee break.
17:00 — Ik ga naar huis. = I go home.

Notice: 'op kantoor' (at the office), not 'in kantoor'.
And 'naar huis' (to home), no article!

steps.example: Zij werkt parttime, drie dagen per week.
steps.exampleEn: She works part-time, three days per week.
steps.note:
Borrowed from English. Also: 'fulltime'.
The Netherlands is the PART-TIME CAPITAL of the world.
Many Dutch people work 4 days (32 hours).

steps.q: 'De vergadering begint om tien uur.' — 'vergadering' means:

steps.s: Het regende, ___ bleef ik thuis.

steps.q: Where does 'namelijk' go in a sentence?
opts: After the verb, inside the clause
steps.ans: After the verb, inside the clause

steps.s: Ik heb een ___ om drie uur. (appointment)
Ik kan niet komen, ik ben {1} ziek. {2} werk ik vandaag thuis.
steps.hint: I'm sick, you see. Therefore I work from home.

steps.q: Why is the Netherlands called the 'part-time capital'?
opts: Many people work 4-day weeks by choice
steps.ans: Many people work 4-day weeks by choice
steps.hint: Work-life balance is a Dutch value

steps.title: Had Al Gedaan
steps.desc: You already know the perfect tense (ik heb gedaan) and the simple past (ik deed). Now meet their older sibling: the past perfect (plusquamperfectum). It describes something that happened BEFORE another past event. Think: 'I had already eaten when he arrived.'
goals: Past perfect with hebben: had + voltooid deelwoord
Past perfect with zijn: was + voltooid deelwoord

steps.title: 📖 Three Past Tenses — The Full Picture
steps.text:
Present perfect (Unit 7):
Ik heb gegeten. = I have eaten. / I ate.
→ Finished action. Most common in speech.

Simple past / imperfectum (Unit 10):
Ik at. = I ate.
→ Stories, descriptions, 'vroeger', writing.

Past perfect / plusquamperfectum (NEW):
Ik had gegeten. = I had eaten.
→ Something that happened BEFORE another past event.

Timeline:
  [had gegeten] ——→ [hij kwam] ——→ [now]
  (earlier past)     (later past)

'Ik had al gegeten toen hij kwam.'
= I had already eaten when he arrived.

The past perfect is the DEEPEST past.
It says: this happened FIRST.

steps.example: Ik had het al gedaan.
steps.exampleEn: I had already done it.
steps.note:
Past perfect with hebben:
had/hadden + voltooid deelwoord.
Same past participle as perfect tense!
'Ik heb gedaan' → 'Ik had gedaan'.

steps.example: Zij was al naar huis gegaan.
steps.exampleEn: She had already gone home.
steps.note:
Past perfect with zijn:
was/waren + voltooid deelwoord.
Same verbs that use 'zijn' in the perfect!
'Ik ben gegaan' → 'Ik was gegaan'.

steps.example: Ik had al gegeten.
steps.exampleEn: I had already eaten.
steps.note:
You know this from earlier lessons!
'Al' = already. Very common with past perfect.
Position: after had/was, before past participle.
'Ik had AL gegeten.'

steps.example: Ik had net gegeten toen hij belde.
steps.exampleEn: I had just eaten when he called.
steps.note:
'Net' = just now/just then.
'Ik had net gegeten.' = I had JUST eaten.
Opposite timing of 'al': al = a while ago, net = moments ago.

steps.example: Toen ik aankwam, was de vergadering al begonnen.
steps.exampleEn: When I arrived, the meeting had already started.
steps.note:
You learned this in Unit 10!
'Toen' = when/then (in the past).
Key connector for past perfect:
'Toen X happened, Y had already happened.'

steps.title: Past Perfect (Plusquamperfectum)
steps.label: had/was + voltooid deelwoord
steps.note:
Formula: had/hadden OR was/waren + past participle.
Same past participle as the regular perfect tense!
Same hebben/zijn split as the regular perfect tense!

steps.title: 📐 When to Use It
steps.text:
The past perfect answers:
'What had ALREADY happened before something else?'

Ik had al gegeten toen hij kwam.
= I had already eaten when he arrived.

Zij was al vertrokken toen ik belde.
= She had already left when I called.

Wij hadden de film al gezien.
= We had already seen the movie.

Pattern: had/was + al + past participle + toen...

You also see it WITHOUT 'toen':
'Ik had het niet verwacht.' = I hadn't expected it.
'Ze waren nog nooit in Nederland geweest.'
= They had never been to the Netherlands.

steps.q: 'Ik had al gegeten toen hij kwam.' — what happened FIRST?
opts: They happened at the same time

steps.s: Zij ___ al naar huis gegaan toen ik belde.

steps.q: The past perfect uses the same past participles as:
steps.hint: heb gewerkt → had gewerkt. Same participle!

steps.s: Ik {1} de brief al {2} toen de baas belde.
steps.hint: I had already written the letter when the boss called.

steps.s: Wij ___ nog nooit in Parijs geweest.
steps.hint: Geweest = zijn verb, wij = plural → waren

steps.q: 'Ik had NET gegeten.' — 'net' means:

steps.s: Toen ik aankwam, ___ de vergadering al begonnen.
steps.hint: Begonnen... zijn verb (change of state) → was

steps.title: Voordat & Nadat
steps.desc: Now that you have the past perfect, you need the connectors that go with it. 'Voordat' (before) and 'nadat' (after) let you describe the ORDER of events. Plus: 'zodra' (as soon as) and 'totdat' (until).

steps.example: Voordat ik naar huis ging, maakte ik het af.
steps.exampleEn: Before I went home, I finished it.
steps.note:
Subordinate conjunction → verb to END.
'Voordat ik naar huis GING, ...'
'Voor' alone = before (preposition, no clause).
'Voordat' = before (with a full clause).

steps.example: Nadat ik had gegeten, ging ik naar bed.
steps.exampleEn: After I had eaten, I went to bed.
steps.note:
Subordinate conjunction → verb to END.
Often paired with past perfect in the nadat-clause:
'Nadat ik HAD GEGETEN, ging ik...'
The nadat-event happened FIRST.

steps.example: Zodra ik thuis ben, bel ik je.
steps.exampleEn: As soon as I'm home, I'll call you.
steps.note:
Subordinate conjunction → verb to END.
'Zodra' = the moment that, as soon as.
Expresses immediate sequence.

steps.example: Ik wacht totdat je klaar bent.
steps.exampleEn: I'll wait until you're ready.
steps.note:
Subordinate conjunction → verb to END.
'Tot' alone = until (preposition).
'Totdat' = until (with full clause).
'Ik wacht totdat je klaar BENT.'

steps.title: 📐 Word Order Reminder
steps.text:
All four are subordinate conjunctions.
The verb goes to the END of the sub-clause:

Voordat ik naar huis GING, ...
Nadat ik had GEGETEN, ...
Zodra ik thuis BEN, ...
Totdat je klaar BENT, ...

The MAIN clause still follows V2:
'Voordat ik ging, MAAKTE ik het af.'
       ↑ sub-clause     ↑ verb 2nd in main clause

When the sub-clause comes FIRST, the main clause
starts with the verb (inversion).
This is the same pattern as omdat/als from Unit 9!

steps.title: 🔑 Nadat + Past Perfect
steps.text:
'Nadat' usually pairs with the past perfect:

Nadat ik had gegeten, ging ik slapen.
= After I had eaten, I went to sleep.

Nadat zij was aangekomen, begon de vergadering.
= After she had arrived, the meeting started.

The nadat-clause = the EARLIER event (past perfect).
The main clause = the LATER event (simple past or perfect).

You CAN use nadat without past perfect,
but with past perfect it's clearer and more precise.

deepDive.title: Voordat + past perfect?
deepDive.text:
You can also use the past perfect in voordat-clauses:

Voordat ik had gegeten, was ik moe.
= Before I had eaten, I was tired.

But this is less common. Usually:
'Voordat' + simple past in the sub-clause
'Nadat' + past perfect in the sub-clause

The logic: nadat describes the completed event
(so past perfect fits). Voordat describes the
event that hadn't happened yet (so simple past fits).

steps.q: 'Nadat ik had gegeten, ging ik slapen.' — what happened first?

steps.s: ___ ik naar huis ging, maakte ik het werk af.

steps.q: In 'Voordat ik naar huis ging', the verb 'ging' is:

steps.s: {1} zij was aangekomen, begon de vergadering. Ik wachtte {2} de baas klaar was.
steps.hint: After she arrived... I waited until...

steps.s: ___ ik thuis ben, bel ik je. (as soon as)

steps.q: 'Nadat' is usually paired with which tense?

steps.s: Nadat ik had ___, ging ik naar bed. (eaten)
steps.hint: Past perfect: had + past participle of eten

steps.title: Solliciteren
steps.desc: Applying for a job in the Netherlands. You'll learn the vocabulary of the hiring process and practice a simple job interview dialogue. Formal register — this is where 'u' comes back.
goals: Formal register: Geachte, Met vriendelijke groet

steps.example: Ik heb gesolliciteerd naar deze baan.
steps.exampleEn: I applied for this job.
steps.note:
'Solliciteren naar' = to apply for.
The noun: 'de sollicitatie' = the application.
'Een sollicitatiebrief' = a cover letter.

steps.example: Stuur je cv naar de werkgever.
steps.exampleEn: Send your CV to the employer.
steps.note:
Het-word. Pronounced 'say-vay'.
In the Netherlands: CV + motivatiebrief
(cover letter) is the standard application.

steps.example: Ik heb morgen een sollicitatiegesprek.
steps.exampleEn: I have a job interview tomorrow.
steps.note:
Het-word. 'Sollicitatiegesprek' = job interview.
Also: 'een goed gesprek' = a good conversation.
Polysemy: conversation AND interview.

steps.example: Heb je ervaring met projectmanagement?
steps.exampleEn: Do you have experience with project management?
steps.note:
De-word. 'Werkervaring' = work experience.
'Ervaren' (adjective) = experienced.
'Een ervaren collega' = an experienced colleague.

steps.example: Welke opleiding heb je gedaan?
steps.exampleEn: What education did you do?
steps.note:
De-word. 'Opleiding' = formal education/degree.
'Een opleiding volgen' = to follow/take a course.
Also used for training programs at work.

steps.example: Het salaris is goed.
steps.exampleEn: The salary is good.
steps.note:
Het-word. Also: 'het loon' (wages).
Dutch job ads often don't list salary.
You negotiate during the gesprek.

steps.title: 💼 Het Sollicitatiegesprek
steps.text:
A typical Dutch job interview:

Werkgever: Vertel eens over uzelf.
= Tell us about yourself.

Jij: Ik heb drie jaar ervaring als programmeur.
Daarvoor heb ik informatica gestudeerd.
= I have three years of experience as a programmer.
Before that, I studied computer science.

Werkgever: Waarom wilt u bij ons werken?
= Why do you want to work with us?

Jij: Ik vind het bedrijf interessant
en ik heb ervaring met dit soort projecten.
= I find the company interesting
and I have experience with this type of project.

Werkgever: Heeft u nog vragen?
= Do you have any questions?

Jij: Ja. Hoeveel vakantiedagen zijn er?
= Yes. How many vacation days are there?

deepDive.title: Dutch interview culture
deepDive.text:
Dutch interviews are relatively informal compared to many countries.
However, they are DIRECT. Expect specific questions about your skills.

Dutch norms:
- Firm handshake, eye contact, arrive ON TIME (not early, not late).
- 'U' at the start, switch to 'je' if they suggest it.
- Be honest about what you CAN and CANNOT do.
- Asking about salary, vacation days, and work-life balance is normal.
- The Dutch value 'nuchterheid' (down-to-earth-ness). Don't oversell.
- Follow up with a short thank-you email.

Vacation: 25 days minimum is standard in the Netherlands.
Most people take 3-4 weeks in summer.

steps.q: 'Solliciteren naar een baan' means:

steps.s: Welke ___ heb je gedaan? (education/training)

steps.q: In a Dutch interview, asking about vacation days is:
steps.hint: Work-life balance is a Dutch value

steps.s: Stuur je ___ naar de werkgever.
Ik heb drie jaar {1} als programmeur. Daarvoor heb ik informatica {2}.
steps.hint: Three years of experience. Before that, I studied.

steps.q: 'Het salaris' is:

steps.title: Op de Vergadering
steps.desc: Time to sit in a Dutch meeting. You'll learn how to discuss, propose, and give your opinion. This lesson introduces the structures 'Ik denk dat...', 'Volgens mij...', and basic meeting vocabulary.
goals: Express opinions: Ik denk dat..., Volgens mij...
Participate in a simple meeting dialogue

steps.example: Ik heb een voorstel.
steps.exampleEn: I have a proposal.
steps.note:
Het-word. 'Een voorstel doen' = to make a proposal.
'Voorstellen' (verb) = to suggest/propose.
Also: 'Mag ik me voorstellen?' = May I introduce myself?

steps.example: Het volgende punt op de agenda.
steps.exampleEn: The next item on the agenda.
steps.note:
Het-word. 'Punt' = point/item.
'De agenda' = the agenda (same word!).
'Goed punt!' = Good point!

steps.example: We moeten een besluit nemen.
steps.exampleEn: We need to make a decision.
steps.note:
Het-word. 'Een besluit nemen' = to make a decision.
'Besluiten' (verb) = to decide.
'Beslissen' = also to decide (more informal).

steps.example: Laten we dit punt bespreken.
steps.exampleEn: Let's discuss this item.
steps.note:
Inseparable verb (be- prefix → not separable).
'Bespreken' = to discuss.
'De bespreking' = the discussion.

steps.title: 💬 Meningen Geven — Giving Opinions
steps.text:
Ik denk dat... = I think that...
Ik vind dat... = I think/feel that...
Volgens mij... = In my opinion...
Misschien kunnen we... = Maybe we could...

Agreeing:
Daar ben ik het mee eens. = I agree with that.
Goed idee! = Good idea!
Dat klopt. = That's correct.

Disagreeing (politely!):
Daar ben ik het niet mee eens. = I disagree.
Ik zie dat anders. = I see it differently.
Ja, maar... = Yes, but...

The Dutch are DIRECT but not rude.
'Ik ben het er niet mee eens' is perfectly polite.
No need to soften — directness is valued.

steps.example: Ik denk dat we meer tijd nodig hebben.
steps.exampleEn: I think that we need more time.
steps.note:
'Denken' = to think (intellectual).
'Dat' introduces subordinate clause → verb to end!
'Ik denk dat we meer tijd nodig HEBBEN.'

steps.example: Volgens mij is dat een goed idee.
steps.exampleEn: In my opinion, that's a good idea.
steps.note:
Literally: 'according to me'.
'Volgens' = according to.
Very common Dutch way to give an opinion.
V2: 'Volgens mij IS dat...' (verb second!).

steps.q: 'Ik denk dat we meer tijd nodig hebben.' — why is 'hebben' at the end?

steps.s: ___ mij is dat een goed idee.
steps.hint: According to me / In my opinion

steps.q: 'Daar ben ik het mee eens' means:
steps.hint: Het eens zijn met = to agree with

steps.s: Ik ___ dat we een besluit moeten nemen.
{1} mij is het een goed {2}. Laten we het bespreken.
steps.hint: In my opinion it's a good proposal. Let's discuss it.

steps.q: In Dutch meeting culture, disagreeing directly is:
steps.hint: Dutch directness is valued in the workplace

steps.title: Werken in Nederland
steps.desc: Dutch work culture is unique. Flat hierarchies, direct communication, lunch at your desk, the Friday borrel, and the world's highest rate of part-time work. This lesson explores what makes working in the Netherlands special.
goals: Understand the borrel, overleg, and parttime culture

steps.title: 🇳🇱 De Nederlandse Werkcultuur
steps.text:
1. Flat hierarchy: de baas is 'Jan', not 'Meneer De Vries'.
Most people call their boss by first name.

2. Direct communication: 'Ik ben het er niet mee eens'
is not rude — it's expected. No beating around the bush.

3. Lunch: brood met kaas, at your desk, in 30 minutes.
No long French lunch breaks.

4. Part-time: the Netherlands is the world champion
of part-time work. Many people work 32 hours (4 days).

5. De borrel: Friday afternoon drinks with colleagues.
Beer, wine, bitterballen. The social glue of Dutch offices.

6. Overleg: the Dutch LOVE meetings (overleg/vergadering).
Everyone gets to speak. Decisions take time.
This is the 'poldermodel' — consensus through discussion.

steps.example: Ga je mee naar de borrel?
steps.exampleEn: Are you coming to the drinks?
steps.note:
THE most Dutch social concept at work.
Friday afternoon = 'borrel time'.
Bitterballen, beer, wine, networking.
'Borrelen' = to have drinks socially.

steps.example: Ik heb nog tien vakantiedagen.
steps.exampleEn: I still have ten vacation days.
steps.note:
De-word. vakantie + dag.
The Dutch get 25 days minimum by law.
Many get more. This is non-negotiable in Dutch culture.

steps.example: We hebben veel overleg op kantoor.
steps.exampleEn: We have a lot of meetings at the office.
steps.note:
You saw this in L2. Repeated because it's SO Dutch.
'Overleggen' = to consult/discuss.
The poldermodel in action: talk until everyone agrees.

steps.example: De werksfeer is hier heel goed.
steps.exampleEn: The work atmosphere here is very good.
steps.note:
De-word. werk + sfeer.
'Sfeer' = atmosphere/vibe.
Dutch workers value 'een goede sfeer' highly.

steps.title: 📖 Lees: Een Dag op Kantoor
steps.text:
Lees het verhaal en beantwoord de vragen.
= Read the story and answer the questions.

Maandag, half negen. Lisa komt op kantoor.
Ze zegt 'hoi' tegen haar collega's en maakt koffie.
Om negen uur heeft ze overleg met haar team.
Ze bespreken het nieuwe project.

Na het overleg beantwoordt Lisa haar e-mails.
Om twaalf uur eet ze een broodje aan haar bureau.
De pauze duurt maar een half uur.

's Middags heeft ze een vergadering met een klant.
De klant is tevreden. Lisa is blij.
Om vijf uur gaat ze naar huis.
Het is maandag — geen borrel vandaag.
Die is op vrijdag.

deepDive.title: Vertaling
deepDive.text:
Monday, half past eight. Lisa arrives at the office.
She says 'hi' to her colleagues and makes coffee.
At nine she has a meeting with her team.
They discuss the new project.

After the meeting, Lisa answers her emails.
At twelve she eats a sandwich at her desk.
The break lasts only half an hour.

In the afternoon she has a meeting with a client.
The client is satisfied. Lisa is happy.
At five she goes home.
It's Monday — no drinks today.
That's on Friday.

steps.q: According to the text, when does Lisa eat lunch?
steps.hint: Om twaalf uur eet ze een broodje aan haar bureau

steps.s: De ___ is op vrijdag.

steps.q: The 'poldermodel' means:
How many vacation days do Dutch workers get minimum?

steps.s: De ___ is hier heel goed. (work atmosphere)

steps.q: In the text, Lisa says 'hoi' to her colleagues. This shows:

steps.title: Unit 11 Review
steps.desc: Let's bring it all together. Work vocabulary, the past perfect, time connectors, and workplace culture. This review tests everything from Unit 11.
goals: Express opinions: Ik denk dat..., Volgens mij...

steps.q: 'Ik had al gegeten toen hij kwam.' — the past perfect is:

steps.s: ___ de vergadering begon, had ik al koffie gedronken.

steps.q: 'Nadat ik had gegeten' — which tense is in the nadat-clause?

steps.s: Zij werkt ___ arts. (as)
{1} ik thuis ben, bel ik je. Ik wacht {2} je klaar bent.
steps.hint: As soon as... I'll wait until...

steps.q: 'Volgens mij is dat een goed idee.' — 'Volgens mij' means:

steps.s: Ik ___ dat we meer tijd nodig hebben.

steps.q: 'De klant heeft altijd gelijk' means:

steps.s: Nadat zij was ___, begon de vergadering. (arrived)
steps.hint: Past perfect with zijn: was + PP

steps.s: Ik {1} drie jaar {2} als programmeur.
steps.hint: I have three years of experience as a programmer.

steps.q: The Dutch 'borrel' is:

steps.s: Wij ___ nog nooit in Parijs geweest. (had — plural)
